text: The surgeon locates the stapled pancreatic duct near the portal vein and divided jejunum. Carefully, the staples closing the duct are removed, revealing the full circumference of the pancreatic duct in preparation for anastomosis.

The surgeon mobilizes the posterior wall of the pancreas from the portal vein and splenic vein at their confluence, preparing for the posterior row anastomosis. A 3-0 V-Loc suture, measuring fifteen centimeters, is used to initiate the anastomosis, chosen for its ease of handling and time efficiency.

The surgeon uses a horizontal mattress suture technique to secure the posterior row anastomosis at the level of the pancreatic duct, carefully avoiding catching the duct itself. The suture is applied using the instrument that provides the best angle, either with the right or left hand, to prevent any gaps and potential pancreatic leaks.
The surgeon maintains most of the staple line intact on the pancreas during the posterior and anterior rows of the anastomosis, ensuring minimal gripping of the pancreatic tissue.

The surgeon ensures that the bowel completely covers both the superior and inferior edges of the pancreas, maintaining the integrity of the staple lines to facilitate the anastomosis and potentially reduce pancreatic leaks.

The surgeon performs a small enterotomy across from the pancreatic duct, confirming entry into the bowel lumen. Photovacryl sutures, alternating between blue-dyed and undyed white, are used for the ductomucosa anastomosis. The pancreatic duct, approximately three to four millimeters in diameter, is divided, and three posterior row sutures and three anterior row sutures are placed.

The surgeon carefully aligns each suture to maintain clarity and prevent tying errors, using robotic magnification to safely take precise bites on the pancreatic duct. After completing the posterior row sutures, corner sutures are placed from the outside of the bowel using photovacryl sutures.

The surgeon ties the sutures from the mucosa through the pancreatic duct towards the pancreas, completing the anterior row before tying the final sutures. Care is taken to avoid ear knots with the coated Vicryl sutures to prevent pancreatic leaks.

The surgeon confirms the correct suture is tied, carefully following the tissue direction to prevent tearing the pancreas. The posterior layer of the ductal mucosa is completed, and the surgeon proceeds to suture the anterior layer using the same type of sutures as the posterior layer.
The surgeon carefully ties the sutures without cutting them too short, ensuring they are tied under no tension to prevent air knots, which could lead to prolonged pancreatic leakage.

The surgeon carefully ties vascular sutures while avoiding tearing the pancreas. The assistant receives the sutures to ensure all are accounted for, preventing an incomplete needle count or leaving a suture inside the patient.
The surgeon completes the ductal mucosa suturing and begins the anterior row using a running layer with 3-0 V-Loc sutures, carefully avoiding tearing the pancreas to prevent leaks.
The surgeon completes the anterior row of sutures and ties both blocks to themselves, ensuring secure closure of the pancreatic jejunostomy.
